narrative: Your law school application was denied because our prediction model estimated a 42% chance that you would not pass the bar exam on your first attempt, based on patterns from thousands of past students' academic and background details. This threshold is set to prioritize admitting those most likely to succeed right away, and unfortunately, your profile tipped toward the higher-risk side. The biggest factor pulling the prediction toward failure was your Year 3 grade decile, which placed you in the bottom tenth of your school—far below the middle-to-upper deciles typical for students who passed the bar. This stood out as the strongest influence, signaling to the model a potential struggle with sustaining strong performance later in your studies. On a more positive note, your LSAT score was a major counterbalance, pushing strongly toward success since it exceeded the average for bar passers by a couple of points, showing your aptitude for the kind of reasoning the exam demands. Your undergraduate GPA also helped your case, coming in close to the typical 3.24 average for successful students and nudging the prediction away from failure, though not enough to offset the Year 3 concern. While your lower-middle family income and full-time study plans align with many who succeed—full-time is the norm for over 90% of bar passers—the model weighed your grades most heavily in this decision. Compared to admitted students, your early Year 1 decile was similarly low, but it was the drop-off by Year 3 that raised the most flags against the profiles of those who typically get approved. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background, just how these key academic markers stacked up. To strengthen a future application, focus on boosting your later-year performance through targeted study habits or tutoring, perhaps retaking the LSAT if possible to solidify that strength, and gathering letters highlighting your resilience. Many students in similar positions have improved their trajectories and succeeded—consider community college courses or bar prep programs to build a stronger record. You're clearly capable, as your LSAT shows, so channeling that into consistent grades could flip this prediction next time. Keep pushing forward; reapplying with clearer evidence of upward momentum often changes the outcome.
